terms.definition: The practice of farming, including growing crops and raising animals for food.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Agriculture began when early people stopped hunting and started growing wheat.
terms.common_errors: Confused with just crops; includes animal husbandry

terms.definition: Relating to the Germanic peoples who settled in England from the 5th century.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From the names of two Germanic tribes: the Angles and Saxons
terms.example_usage: Anglo-Saxon kingdoms spread across much of England before the Norman invasion.
terms.common_errors: Thinking Anglo-Saxons were a single unified group

terms.definition: The study of the past through examination of physical remains such as buildings, tools, and bones.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Archaeology has helped us learn about Roman towns buried underground.
terms.common_errors: Confused with palaeontology, which studies fossils

terms.definition: A connective used to introduce a reason or explanation for why something happened.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Romans invaded because they wanted Britains resources.
terms.common_errors: Using because to state an outcome rather than a reason

terms.definition: Unfairly favouring one side or viewpoint over another, affecting how reliably a source presents the past.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Roman writer may have been biased against the Celts.
terms.common_errors: Assuming a biased source has no value

terms.definition: A period roughly 2500-800 BC in Britain when people first used bronze for tools and weapons.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: Named after bronze, the alloy of copper and tin
terms.example_usage: During the Bronze Age, people began to trade metals across long distances.
terms.common_errors: Thinking it started at the same time everywhere

terms.definition: The reason why something happened; what made an event or change take place.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: One cause of the Roman invasion was the desire for new lands.
terms.common_errors: Confusing a cause with a consequence

terms.definition: The arrangement of events in the order in which they occurred, from earliest to most recent.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Understanding chronology helps us see how the Stone Age came before the Bronze Age.
terms.common_errors: Confused with timeline, which is a visual representation

terms.definition: An independent city with its own government that controls the surrounding area, common in ancient Greece.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Athens was a powerful city-state known for inventing democracy.
terms.common_errors: Thinking a city-state is the same as a capital city

terms.definition: An advanced society with organised government, culture, writing, and technology.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Latin civilis, relating to citizens
terms.example_usage: The ancient Egyptian civilisation lasted for over 3,000 years.
terms.common_errors: Implying societies without writing are not civilisations

terms.definition: The act of taking control of a place or people by military force.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Norman conquest of England began with the Battle of Hastings in 1066.

terms.definition: Something that happens as a result of an action or event; the outcome.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: One consequence of the Roman roads was faster trade across Britain.
terms.common_errors: Confusing consequence with cause

terms.definition: To confirm or support a claim by comparing it with other evidence.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: We tried to corroborate the diary entry by checking other sources.
terms.common_errors: Thinking one matching source is enough

terms.definition: Standards or rules used to judge something, such as whether an event is historically significant.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: We used five criteria to decide if the invention was historically significant.
terms.common_errors: Using criteria as singular; the singular is criterion

terms.definition: A change that results from an action or event; what happened because of something.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The effect of the plague was a shortage of workers across England.
terms.common_errors: Confused with affect (verb)

terms.definition: A group of countries or regions controlled by one ruler or governing power.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Roman Empire stretched from Britain to North Africa.
terms.common_errors: Thinking empires are always bad or always good

terms.definition: To carefully consider evidence and make a judgement about its usefulness, reliability, or importance.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: We evaluated the source by considering who wrote it and why.
terms.common_errors: Confusing evaluate with describe

terms.definition: One of several things that combine to cause an event or bring about a result.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The distance from Rome was one factor in Britain being hard to control.
terms.common_errors: Confused with the mathematical meaning

terms.definition: A system in which people or groups are ranked one above another according to power or status.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: In ancient Egypt, the pharaoh was at the top of the hierarchy.

terms.definition: A person who studies and writes about the past using evidence from sources.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Historians disagree about why the Roman Empire fell.
terms.common_errors: Thinking historians simply memorise facts

terms.definition: Having great value, meaning, or effect; worth remembering or studying.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: It is important to study primary sources to understand what really happened.

terms.definition: Not having all the parts or information; sources that do not give the full picture.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Our evidence about the Stone Age is incomplete because so few objects survived.
terms.common_errors: Thinking incomplete evidence is worthless

terms.definition: An explanation or understanding of the past based on evidence, which may differ between people.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Some historians have a different interpretation of why the Vikings came to Britain.
terms.common_errors: Thinking there is only one correct interpretation

terms.definition: An armed force entering another country or region to take control of it.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The Roman invasion of Britain began in AD 43 under Emperor Claudius.
terms.common_errors: Confused with immigration, which is peaceful

terms.definition: A period roughly 800 BC to AD 43 in Britain when iron replaced bronze for tools and weapons.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: Named after iron, the defining metal
terms.example_usage: Iron Age people in Britain lived in hillforts for protection.
terms.common_errors: Thinking the Iron Age ended at the same time everywhere

terms.definition: A system of channels or pipes used to bring water to crops, especially in dry areas.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The ancient Egyptians used irrigation from the River Nile to grow food.
terms.common_errors: Confused with drainage, which removes water

terms.definition: To form an opinion about something based on careful thought and evidence.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: We need to judge how reliable the source is before using it.
terms.common_errors: Confused with the legal meaning of a judge

terms.definition: A territory ruled by a king or queen, with its own laws and government.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Anglo-Saxon England was divided into several separate kingdoms.

terms.definition: Continuing for a long time or having effects that remain over many years.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Old English laestan, to endure
terms.example_usage: The Roman roads had a lasting effect on travel in Britain.

terms.definition: A causal phrase meaning one event or action brought about another.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The discovery of gold led to a rush of settlers moving west.
terms.common_errors: Implying a simple one-cause explanation

terms.definition: Extending over a lengthy period of time, often years or decades.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The long-term effect of Roman rule was a network of roads still used today.
terms.common_errors: Confusing long-term causes with short-term triggers

terms.definition: Worth remembering because of being special, unusual, or historically important.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The first Moon landing in 1969 was one of the most memorable events in history.

terms.definition: Relating to the people from Normandy in northern France who conquered England in 1066.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Norman castles were built across England to control the population.
terms.common_errors: Thinking Normans were French; they descended from Vikings

terms.definition: Worthy of attention or remark; standing out because of importance or interest.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Boudicca is a notable figure in the history of Roman Britain.

terms.definition: The final result or consequence of an event, decision, or process.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The outcome of the Battle of Hastings was Norman control of England.

terms.definition: Not complete, or showing favouritism towards one side; a source that only tells part of the story.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The account was partial because it only included the winners point of view.
terms.common_errors: Not distinguishing between the two meanings: incomplete vs biased

terms.definition: A length of time in history with shared characteristics, such as the Tudor or Victorian period.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The medieval period lasted from about 1066 to 1485.
terms.common_errors: Thinking a period has an exact start and end date

terms.definition: A particular way of looking at events, shaped by experience, beliefs, or position in society.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Latin perspicere, to look through
terms.example_usage: The Viking perspective on their raids was very different from the monks perspective.
terms.common_errors: Thinking perspective is the same as opinion

terms.definition: The title given to the ruler of ancient Egypt, who held absolute power and was considered divine.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Tutankhamun became pharaoh when he was only about nine years old.

terms.definition: Belonging to the time before written records, studied through archaeology rather than documents.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Prehistoric people left behind stone tools but no written records.
terms.common_errors: Thinking prehistoric means when dinosaurs lived

terms.definition: In history, first-hand or from the time being studied; created during or close to the events.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: A Roman coin is a primary piece of evidence about what Romans valued.
terms.common_errors: Thinking primary means most important rather than from the time

terms.definition: The origin and history of a source, including who created it, when, where, and why.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From French provenir, to come from
terms.example_usage: Knowing the provenance of a source helps us decide how reliable it is.
terms.common_errors: Confusing provenance with just the date

terms.definition: The explanation for why something happened; the thinking or motivation behind an event.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: One reason the Romans built roads was to move their army quickly.

terms.definition: To keep an event or person in your mind; to acknowledge past events and people involved.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: We remember the soldiers who fought in the World Wars every November.

terms.definition: What happened because of an action or event; the outcome or consequence.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: As a result of the invasion, the language of England began to change.

terms.definition: Relating to the ancient civilisation based in Rome that built an empire across Europe and beyond.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Roma, the Latin name for Rome
terms.example_usage: Roman soldiers built Hadrians Wall to protect the northern border.
terms.common_errors: Assuming all Romans came from Italy

terms.definition: In history, created after the event by someone who was not there; based on others evidence.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The documentary is a secondary source because it was made long after the war.
terms.common_errors: Thinking secondary means less useful

terms.definition: Lasting for or relating to a brief period of time, often days, weeks, or months.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: A short-term effect of the plague was the closing of markets and fairs.
terms.common_errors: Confusing short-term effects with long-term consequences

terms.definition: The importance or meaning of an event, person, or development in the broader sweep of history.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The significance of Magna Carta is that it limited the power of the king.
terms.common_errors: Thinking significance is fixed; it can change over time

terms.definition: The earliest known period of human history, when people used tools and weapons made from stone.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: Named after stone, the primary tool material
terms.example_usage: Stone Age people made sharp flint tools for cutting and scraping.
terms.common_errors: Thinking Stone Age people were unintelligent

terms.definition: A connective meaning for that reason or as a consequence of what has been stated.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The harvest failed; therefore, many people went hungry that winter.
terms.common_errors: Used without having established a clear cause first

terms.definition: The buying, selling, or exchanging of goods and services between people or countries.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Trade between Rome and Britain brought new foods and materials.

terms.definition: A social group of families or communities linked by shared customs, language, and leadership.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Celtic tribes in Iron Age Britain each had their own chief.
terms.common_errors: Using tribe to imply primitiveness

terms.definition: An event that directly sets off a larger event, often the final cause in a chain.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand was the trigger for World War One.
terms.common_errors: Thinking the trigger is the only cause

terms.definition: Not planned or expected; consequences that people did not foresee when they took an action.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: An unintended consequence of building the railway was the growth of seaside towns.
terms.common_errors: Confusing unintended with unimportant

terms.definition: Relating to the Scandinavian seafarers who raided, traded, and settled across Europe from the 8th to 11th centuries.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Viking longships could travel across oceans and up shallow rivers.
terms.common_errors: Thinking Vikings only raided; they were also traders and settlers

terms.definition: A question word used to ask about the reasons or causes behind events in the past.
terms.subject: History
terms.example_usage: Why did people in the past build castles on hilltops?

terms.definition: A system of recording language using symbols or letters; one of the key features of early civilisations.
terms.subject: History
terms.etymology: From Old English writan, to scratch
terms.example_usage: The invention of writing allowed ancient Egyptians to keep records of their laws.
terms.common_errors: Forgetting that writing systems vary enormously
